- **finding: singularity appears as dirac δ peak in d²i/dv²** — Theoretical result that the tunneling current singularity manifests as a Dirac delta function.
- **finding: singularity enhanced when v_n → v_d (nested bands)** — The amplitude of the singularity grows as tip and sample velocities become equal.
- **finding: soc weakly modifies spin splitting** — DFT results show that inclusion of SOC changes ΔNR only slightly, mainly enabling coupling to ferroelectric polarization.
- **finding: square‑root divergence in conductance** — Observation that dI/dV diverges as √(V‑V*) near the elastic tunneling threshold.
- **finding: step‑size repeatability < 5 %** — Step‑size repeatability is typically better than 5 % at a given frequency and voltage.
- **finding: stm image may be qualitatively different for different tunneling polarity or sample doping** — Result of calculations on GaAs(110) showing polarity/doping dependence of the STM contrast.
- **finding: stm is rather insensitive to the position of the surface layer relative to underlying layers** — Conclusion drawn from calculations on Au(110) that STM images change little with surface‑layer shifts.
- **finding: symmetric bands at charge neutrality** — At ν = 0 the flat bands are symmetric around the Fermi level and separated by ~35 meV.
- **finding: tangency produces inverse‑square‑root singularity** — The analysis shows that tangency between tip Fermi line and sample dispersion generically yields an inverse‑square‑root singularity in dI/dV.
- **finding: w₀/w₁ ≈ 0.8 yields δ ratio ~10⁻²** — Theoretical ratio of tunneling parameters leads to a small jump magnitude in dI/dV.
- **finding: γ‑point stretching with filling** — Γ‑point states shift dramatically with filling, turning a peak at ν = –4 into a dip at ν = 4.
- **finding: δnr ≫ δsoc** — Non‑relativistic spin splitting is much larger than the SOC‑induced splitting.
- **fine tuning of optical transition energy via interlayer distance** — Interlayer distance modulation enables precise control of optical transition energies.
- **first observation of an electrically switchable p‑wave magnet** — The reported results constitute the first experimental observation of an electrically switchable p‑wave magnet.
- **first observation of an electrically switchable p‑wave magnet** — Experimental demonstration that a p‑wave magnet can be toggled by an electric field.
- **first observation of nrss of magnon bands in crsb** — The study reports the first example of non‑reciprocal spin splitting of magnon bands in a metallic altermagnet.
- **flat bands flat within ~4 mev** — Extracted band dispersion shows a bandwidth of about 4 meV, indicating extremely flat bands.
- **flat vdw plateau forms on tip apex** — The transferred layers self‑assemble into a flat plateau whose size ranges from 50 nm to 1 µm.
- **flat‑band bandwidth reduction finding** — Observation that the flat‑band bandwidth shrinks by ~17 % at 0.68 GPa.
- **flat‑band shift finding** — The flat band at K_T shifts downward with increasing ν and reaches the Fermi level near ν≈0.6.
- **flat‑band splitting into hubbard bands** — Flat bands of MATBG split into Hubbard bands well above the ordering temperature.
- **flat‑band width 70 mev** — Maximum width of the flat band at Γ for θ_TBG=1.2° is 70 meV.
- **forces up to 50 nn did not damage hbn or graphene** — Empirical observation that tip forces ≤ 50 nN are safe for the studied 2D materials.
- **fresnel reflection coefficient vanishes for s polarization** — Result obtained from the quasistatic analysis of the sandwich.
- **fwhm ≈ 0.2 ev for θ ≈ 0.5°, predicted ≤ 0.1 ev for θ = 0** — Measured and theoretically predicted spectral width of the resonant emission.
- **g_incoh provides convenient scale for tunneling current** — Statement that the incoherent differential conductance G_incoh is a useful reference scale for QTM tunneling results.
- **gaas(110) conduction‑band stm does not reflect topography** — Conduction‑band tunneling on GaAs(110) emphasizes Ga atoms and yields images not simply related to surface geometry.
- **gate‑tunable resonant tunneling in double bilayer graphene** — Observation of gate‑controlled resonant peaks in double‑bilayer devices.
- **gaussian peak 1** — First symmetric Gaussian component used to fit linecut B.
- **gaussian peak 2** — Second symmetric Gaussian component used to fit linecut B.
- **geometric capacitance contributes ~70 % near breakdown voltage** — At high bias the geometric capacitance accounts for roughly 70 % of the total capacitance.
- **good agreement of coupling magnitudes and twist‑angle dependence** — Extracted electron‑phason and electron‑optical couplings match previous data in magnitude and twist‑angle trend.
- **gw approximation overestimates plasmon satellite energies and intensities** — Comparison with microscopic calculations indicates that the GW method predicts too large satellite energies and amplitudes.
- **hartree‑driven band stretching near γ point** — Momentum‑dependent stretching of flat bands observed near Γ, varying with carrier filling.
- **hartree‑stretching finding** — The Hartree potential generated by AA‑localized electrons explains the Γ‑point stretching.
- **heat generated from the actuators is in the sub‑microwatt range** — Because the ceramics act like perfect capacitors, their dissipation is negligible.
- **heating the cantilever to 50 °c enhances adhesion through improved van‑der‑waals bonding** — Observed increase in adhesion when the cantilever is heated.
- **heavy‑light transition angle defined by θ_hl(ν)** — Sigmoid fit yields a proxy angle where heavy‑electron intensity drops by 80 %.
- **high peak‑valley ratio after first etch** — Peak‑valley ratio of 18.5 achieved for devices as small as 0.03 µm² with peak voltage <0.1 V.
- **high precision angular motion benefit** — m° precision leads to high resolution and good signal‑to‑noise in angle‑dependent measurements.
- **high resolution imaging** — FM‑AFM with a high‑Q cantilever can achieve sub‑nanometer spatial resolution.
- **high resonance frequencies** — All three STM models exhibit fairly high resonance frequencies.
- **higher stepping pulse voltage required at 4 k** — At 4 K stepping pulses typically need >40 V, whereas at ambient only 5–30 V are sufficient.
- **high‑bias d2i/dv2 peak tracing plasmon dispersion** — Peak in the second derivative of conductance at high bias whose bias V*(θ) follows the momentum‑resolved plasmon dispersion.
- **horizontal features indicate heavy‑electron flat bands** — Extended horizontal intensity in momentum maps corresponds to heavy‑electron bands.
- **hourglass feature in θ‑v_b plane** — Curved feature arising from quantum capacitance effects when the nesting condition follows V_b = ±ħ v_F K_D θ.
- **hubbard‑band evolution of heavy f‑electrons reproduced** — Calculated spectral functions reproduce the evolution of Hubbard bands with filling.
- **hyperbolic di/dv peaks reveal true tangency** — Intensity of hyperbolic dI/dV peaks distinguishes the correct tangency point due to vanishing tunneling matrix elements for antiparallel alignment.
- **hyperbolic intersection yields a current maximum at δε = 0** — When v_F(probe) < v_F(sample) the current peaks at zero energy offset and d²I/dV² shows a minimum.
- **i ∝ |φ−φ₀| for sharp dirac cone** — When v₀ < v_D, the tunneling current scales linearly with the absolute band‑offset deviation.
- **identical spectra with different contact modalities** — Continuous contact and woodpecker modalities produce practically identical spectra.
- **identical spectra with different tips** — Measurements with Tip D and Tip E give almost identical dI/dV spectra despite different tip shapes.
- **identification of hubbard bands with cascading behaviour** — Two classes of peaks identified as Hubbard bands that shift systematically with filling.
- **identification of momentum‑square‑dependent zeeman term g₄₃** — A new Zeeman contribution proportional to momentum squared was observed.
- **identification of penetration zeeman term g_p** — A smaller Zeeman contribution arising from wave‑function penetration into the AlGaAs barrier was observed.
- **iets can qualitatively distinguish qsl types** — Theoretical result that IETS spectra show distinct features for different QSLs.
- **improved resolution using hbn tunneling dielectric** — Integration of few‑layer hBN increased bias range threefold (±2.5 V) and sharpened spectral features.
- **improved resolution with high q cantilever** — Using a high‑Q cantilever in vacuum dramatically improves AFM imaging resolution.
- **improvement of several orders of magnitude in response to disturbances** — Result showing a large reduction in tip‑sample distance response.
- **increase of twist angle leads to peak splitting and vanishing** — Observed that larger θ causes the resonant peak to split and disappear near θ≈ 3°.
- **individual rped patterns exhibit threefold symmetry** — Measured RPED patterns for fixed helicity and Néel direction show threefold symmetry rather than the sixfold expected from the crystal space group.
- **inelastic scattering between electrons and plasmon on different sides of the tunnel junction** — Result that electrons on tip and sample can exchange plasmons, contributing to the tunneling current.
- **inelastic scattering bridges momentum mismatch at large twist angles** — Result that phonon emission enables tunneling when tip and sample momenta do not overlap.
- **inelastic tunneling dominates at large twist angles** — When the twist angle exceeds ~5°, momentum mismatch forces tunneling to proceed via phonon emission.
- **inelastic tunneling dominates for v_b < v*** — Conclusion that the tunneling current below V* is entirely due to inelastic processes.
- **inelastic tunneling into dispersive bands produces weaker features than into the flat‑band manifold** — Observation that the normalized inelastic tunneling rate and DOS make dispersive‑band signatures weaker.
- **inelastic tunneling probes collective‑mode dispersions** — Inelastic tunneling current reveals phonon dispersions along specific momentum lines.
- **intensity matches w0/w1 = 0.6** — Momentum dependence of the conduction‑band intensity agrees with theoretical curves for w0/w1 = 0.6.
- **intensity of d²i/dv² features determined by sample wave functions for flat bands** — For flat bands the twist‑angle dependence of d²I/dV² peak intensity reflects only the electronic wave functions.
- **intensity of plasmon satellites drops near γ point** — Observed rapid decrease of satellite peak intensity when approaching the γ point.
- **interacting dispersion matches qtm spectrum** — Renormalized band structure quantitatively agrees with measured d²I/dV².
- **interactions reshape band structure** — Measured bands deviate substantially from the non‑interacting BM prediction, highlighting the role of interactions.
- **interference suppression observed** — The tunneling amplitude is strongly reduced for twist angles smaller than the commensurate angle.
- **interferometric contrast > 500 ‰ recommended** — Recommended interferometric contrast higher than 500 ‰ for reliable optics alignment.
- **interlayer coupling negligible at small bias** — Theoretical result that interlayer electron‑plasmon coupling can be ignored when |eV| ≪ ħv_D|q|.
- **interlayer coupling remains non-zero at long wavelength** — Interlayer electron‑phonon coupling does not vanish as q→0.
- **interlayer electron‑plasmon coupling yields small corrections** — Corrections from interlayer coupling are quantitatively small at low bias.
- **interlayer epc appears in first‑order process** — Interlayer EPC contributes directly to tunnelling without a virtual intermediate state.
- **interlayer epc dominates for the ta mode** — Experimental and theoretical analysis shows the TA phonon couples more strongly across layers than within a layer.
- **interlayer epc increases with decreasing qm, independent of qm for max bond stretch** — Interlayer EPC intensifies as qM decreases, because the maximal bond stretch equals the full ZPM.
- **interlayer mechanism is much stronger for acoustic ta mode** — Parametric analysis indicates a pronounced interlayer contribution for the TA acoustic phonon.
- **intralayer epc contribution grows as twist angle decreases** — Averaged intralayer coupling becomes stronger when the two graphene sheets are more closely aligned.
- **intralayer epc dominates for the la mode** — LA phonon coupling is stronger within each graphene layer than between layers.
- **intralayer mechanism dominates for optical to and lo modes** — For optical phonons, the intralayer EPC is the leading contribution.
- **intrinsic mass values** — Intrinsic mass m0 values are of order 0.001–0.003 kg (a few grams).
- **inverted sign of linear dependence on hole side** — When tuned to negative back‑gate voltage, the slope of the linear dependence reverses.
- **in‑layer epc strength proportional to zpm amplitude, vanishes as q→0** — In‑layer EPC scales with zero‑point motion and disappears for vanishing momentum transfer.
- **in‑situ manipulation for twistronics** — Showed that twist angle can be tuned after device fabrication.
- **in‑situ twistable bilayer graphene** — Real‑time control of the twist angle in a bilayer graphene device.
- **isospin magnetism and spin‑polarized superconductivity in bernal bilayer graphene** — Coexistence of isospin magnetic order and spin‑polarized superconductivity.
- **kink magnitude matches theoretical estimate** — Measured size of the kink agrees with the calculated inelastic tunneling rate (~4×10⁻³).
- **kinks are two orders of magnitude weaker than plasmon satellites** — Quantitative comparison showing the kink signal is ~100× smaller than the plasmon‑satellite signal.
- **kinks in di/dv are relatively weak features** — Observed kinks are about two orders of magnitude weaker than central‑band plasmon satellites.
- **kitaev quantum spin liquid behaviour in honeycomb magnet** — Observation reported in the Proximate Kitaev QSL paper.
- **la mode contribution is absent in measured epc** — Experimental observation of a missing longitudinal acoustic mode.
- **large amplitude requirement** — Large oscillation amplitudes are needed to prevent cantilever instabilities and to reduce feedback‑loop noise.
- **large magnetic cd for light helicity parallel to néel vector** — Theoretical RPED calculation shows a strong magnetic CD when the light helicity is parallel to L.
- **large magnetic cd signal in rped for parallel geometry** — Observation that a strong magnetic CD contrast appears even when conventional XMCD absorption is zero.
- **large mcd in rped patterns of mnte** — Observation of a pronounced magnetic circular dichroism in RPED patterns of MnTe in parallel geometry.
- **large spin splitting in odd‑parity magnets** — Spin splitting in odd‑parity magnets can reach the eV scale, enabling enhanced electrical readout.
- **larger amplitude is required for stable feedback and fast scan speed.** — Experimental observation that amplitudes larger than the minimal‑noise value are needed for practical scanning.
- **larger dots exhibit higher peak current** — Observation that dots with larger diameter show larger peak currents in the I‑V curves.
- **larger‑angle twist θ_tbg = 1.2°** — Step‑function fit of the conduction‑band edge gives a twist angle of 1.2 degrees.
- **layer polarization finding** — Demonstration that d²I/dV² amplitudes are stronger when the MLG lattice aligns with the top TBG layer, revealing layer‑specific wavefunction weight.
- **layers – 4 make sense for grounding plane** — Observation that four layers are appropriate to provide a grounding plane.
- **ld domain shows memory effect** — Linear‑dichroism domains retain their orientation after field and temperature cycles.
- **ldos gain/loss after h₂o adsorption** — Spatial LDOS map shows regions of increased and decreased density after water adsorption.
- **lever-arm effect enhances temperature smearing** — Temperature smearing of dI/dV singularity is enhanced by factor |v_T(p**) / v_S(p**)| ≫ 1.
- **lifetime scales as τ ∝ 1/(e‑ef)** — Interpretation of the Apeak trend suggests a 1/E scaling rather than the Fermi‑liquid 1/E².
- **light and heavy electronic character at different parts of momentum space** — Spatial separation of c‑electron‑like and f‑electron‑like behavior in MATBG.
- **limited bandwidth of am‑afm** — Maximum usable bandwidth ≈0.5 Hz for Q=50000 and f₀≈50 kHz, making AM‑AFM too slow for many applications.
- **linear 1/apeak versus (e‑ef) dependence** — Data fit shows a linear relationship better than quadratic forms.
- **linear dependence of conductance step height on gate voltage** — Height of TA and ZA conductance steps varies linearly with back‑gate voltage.
- **linear dependence of photocurrent on laser power** — Zero‑bias photocurrent scales linearly with 532 nm laser power.
- **linear dichroism finding** — Linear dichroism shows a sharp jump at the helimagnetic transition temperature.
- **local chemical potential measured using atomic defect quantum dot** — The atomic defect quantum dot provides a direct probe of the local chemical potential µ of the graphene layers.
- **logarithmic di/dv behavior from hyperbolic intersection** — For a sample Dirac point, the tunneling current shows dI/dV ∼ ln|V−V**| due to a single hyperbola branch.
- **long‑lived spin waves in metallic antiferromagnet** — Demonstration of extended spin‑wave lifetimes in a metallic antiferromagnet.
- **lower bound on elastic scattering length** — From the angular width of the tunneling peak, a lower bound of >150 nm on the elastic scattering length is extracted.
- **lowest filling factor = 1** — Observation of the lowest Landau level filling.
- **low‑bias feature (~65 mv) finding** — Feature at bias ≈ ħωp/e ≈ 65 mV attributed to phonon‑assisted tunnelling.
- **low‑energy defect states observed** — Defect‑assisted tunnelling reveals low‑energy defect levels in carbon‑doped hBN.
- **low‑temperature qtf properties** — At 4.2 K the Q factor increases about tenfold while the resonance frequency decreases by several tens of Hz.
- **magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene as a topological heavy fermion problem** — Identification of a heavy-fermion-like description with topological features.
- **magnetoelectric response from domain rearrangement in nii2 and coi2** — Domain rearrangement induces magnetoelectric effects in triangular‑lattice helimagnets.
- **magnon gap of 13 mev** — Low‑energy INS with Eᵢ=50 meV resolves a gap of 13(2) meV at the magnetic zone centre.
- **magnon gap of 13 mev measured by ins** — INS spectra at 5 K reveal a magnon gap of 13(2) meV.
- **main finding on cd‑rped applicability to altermagnets** — Our main findings are valid for any altermagnet and lay the ground for making CD‑RPED a powerful technique for magnetic characterization of altermagnet candidate materials.
- **matrix element is the same for normal and superconducting transitions** — The tunnelling matrix element does not change between the two states.
- **matrix elements factorize into independent contributions of the two spinors** — Result that tunneling matrix elements separate into spinor contributions.
- **matrix elements involve one momentum close to the k points** — Observation that tunneling matrix elements are dominated by momenta near K points.
- **mcd independent of absolute néel orientation** — The MCD pattern depends only on the scalar product L·q, not on the absolute direction of the Néel vector.
- **mcd pattern identical to (a‑b) up to a constant factor r** — The magnetic CD pattern equals the structural difference pattern multiplied by R (≈ −0.84 at L₃, +0.61 at L₂).
- **mcd proportional to xmcd of single sublattice** — Photon‑energy dependence of MCD is approximately proportional to the XMCD of a single magnetic sublattice.
- **mcd sign reversal between l3 and l2 edges** — The magnetic circular dichroism changes sign when moving from the Mn L3 to the Mn L2 resonance.
- **measured c6 symmetry of potential** — Approximate six‑fold symmetry of the potential around the moiré centre.
- **measured lifetime of two‑dimensional electrons** — Lifetime extracted from tunnelling spectroscopy (Murphy et al., 1995).
- **measured phonon branches match calculated graphite phonon spectrum** — Acoustic (TA, ZA) and optical (LO, TO, ZO) branches identified agree with theory.
- **measured phonon dispersion and electron‑phason coupling** — Phonon band structure and electron‑phason interaction observed with the QTM (Inbar et al., 2023).
- **measured piezo conversion ratios** — Experimental determination of Å/V (or nm/V) conversion factors for Z and X/Y tubes at 300 K and 4.2 K.
- **measured potential amplitude ≈60 mv** — Peak‑to‑peak potential amplitude observed at zero carrier density.
- **mechanical gap ≈4 mev in bernal bilayer graphene** — Mechanical gap of ~4 meV measured for the layer‑antisymmetric TA mode in Bernal bilayer graphene.
- **method to extract the w₀/w₁ ratio from differential conductance** — Proposed analysis of tunneling matrix elements that yields the intra‑/inter‑sublattice tunneling ratio.
- **mfm feedback loop established** — MFM feedback loop successfully set at +2 Hz and tip retracted ~50 nm for measurement.
- **minimal noise occurs for amplitudes on the order of the tip‑sample force range (~1 å).** — Result reported by Giessibl that the lowest vertical noise is obtained at amplitudes comparable to the force range.
- **minimum contact resistance of 33 ω** — The lowest contact resistance observed after deoxidation and direct contact measurement.
- **minimum twist angle 1°–2° for optical phonons** — Derivation that optical phonons (~200 meV) require a twist angle of roughly 1°–2° to satisfy the inelastic tunneling condition.
- **mirror loss total > 0.56** — Total mirror losses exceed 0.56 (including Au, Ag, and ITO mirrors).
- **mlg probe dispersion enables conversion of vbb to energy shift** — The probe dispersion allows the bias voltage to be translated into the energy shift φ between MLG and TBG bands.
- **mlg tip plasmon contributes weak background to differential conductance** — The plasmon mode of the doped tip yields only a smooth, weak background in the differential conductance.
- **mobility 3.04×10⁶ cm²/v·s** — Result obtained from transport analysis.
- **mobility changes** — Variations of carrier mobility linked to STO phase transitions.
- **moiré potential amplitude large (52–62 mv)** — The extracted moiré potential amplitude is between 52 mV and 62 mV and varies by only ~10 % with filling.
- **moiré potential changes minimally with carrier density** — Amplitude of φ(x,y) varies by only ~10 % when the filling factor is tuned.
- **moiré potential decays rapidly with distance from the interface** — Measurements with defects D1 and D2 show a strong reduction of φ over ~1 nm.
- **moiré potential exhibits approximate c6 symmetry** — Measured φ(x,y) maps show six‑fold rotational symmetry that changes little with carrier density.
- **moiré potential symmetry close to c6 with minor c3 component** — High‑symmetry points reveal a dominant six‑fold symmetry with a small three‑fold contribution.
- **momentum and energy resolution of qtm** — The QTM can simultaneously resolve momentum and energy of excitations in 2D QSLs.
- **momentum resolution enables extraction of spinon parameters** — Twist‑angle–controlled momentum resolution allows determination of spinon effective mass and Fermi velocity.
- **momentum‑ and energy‑resolved spectral function** — Direct measurement of the full spectral function of a 2D electron gas.
- **momentum‑conserving tunnelling restored at finite twist angle** — Observation that a finite twist angle revives a large tunnelling current after it collapses at θ = 0° with bias.
- **momentum‑dependent spin splitting in collinear antiferromagnets** — Collinear antiferromagnetic order produces momentum‑dependent spin splitting.
- **momentum‑space splitting along (h,0,2.85) indicates altermagnetism** — Constant‑energy slices at 180 meV show a 0.15 r.l.u. splitting reproduced by J₁₁≈1.8 meV, a hallmark of altermagnetism.
- **mott states supporting plasmons** — Mott states with finite‑width Hubbard bands could support plasmon excitations.
- **multiple peaks in di/dv** — Simulated dI/dV map shows multiple conductance peaks when the tip Fermi line touches Bogoliubov equal‑energy contours.
- **naive layer polarization inconsistent with data** — Simple layer‑polarization estimates do not match the experimental spectra.
- **negative differential conductance in graphene transistors** — Observation of NDC arising from resonant tunnelling in graphene.
- **negative differential resistance observed** — Sharp drop in current leading to negative differential resistance near certain bias.
- **negative inverse compressibility finding** — Negative inverse compressibility coincides with charge reshuffling from light to heavy electrons.
- **negative‑bias dip in d2i/dv2 tracing plasmon dispersion** — Dip in the second derivative of conductance at negative bias that follows the plasmon dispersion.
- **negligible interlayer coupling at small bias** — Interlayer electron‑plasmon coupling effects on conductance are negligible at low bias.
- **nematicity and competing orders in superconducting magic‑angle graphene** — Nematic electronic order intertwined with superconductivity.
- **new heavy band appearance finding** — When ν≈1 a new heavy band appears at an energy U above the existing flat band, restarting the cycle.
- **new simulation reveals secrets of exotic polarons** — First‑principles simulation that uncovers properties of hole and exciton polarons in hBN.
- **no interference between intra‑ and interlayer couplings** — The π/2 phase shift prevents interference terms between the two electron‑phonon contributions.
- **nodal point observed at v_b = 0 for p_y pairing** — Positive and negative branches meet at zero bias, closing the gap and producing a nodal point.
- **nodal superconducting gap in moiré graphene** — Experimental detection of a nodal gap structure.
- **noise worsens after one week** — Empirical observation that the noise level degrades after a week of continuous measurement.
- **nonzero mcd in mnte** — Calculated RPED shows a sizable magnetic circular dichroism in MnTe, indicating broken time‑reversal symmetry.
- **non‑monotonic shift of γ peak correlates with inverse compressibility sign change** — The Γ‑point dI/dV peak moves down with filling, reverses direction where the inverse compressibility changes sign.
- **non‑relativistic contribution dominates spin splitting even with soc** — DFT result that SOC adds only minor additional splitting, leaving the odd‑parity non‑relativistic spin polarization as the main effect.
- **normalized mcd approximates the xmcd of one sublattice** — Dividing the MCD pattern by the (A‑B) structural pattern yields an estimate of the XMCD spectrum of a single sublattice.
- **noticeable improvement in stm performance** — Experimental confirmation that the optimized design yields better STM operation.
- **numerical results for strong gate screening** — Computed |M|² for 1.07° TBG with d_g = 1 nm showing enhanced inelastic tunneling.
- **observation of a new ~15 mev excitation** — Spectral feature not captured by existing models, suggesting an extra degree of freedom.
- **observation of chiral and slow plasmons** — Experimental detection of chiral and unusually slow plasmon modes in twisted bilayer graphene.
- **observation of coulomb blockade and staircase** — Clear Coulomb blockade and staircase features observed for the 450 nm quantum dot.
- **observation of cpge in nii2 spin helices** — Detection of a sizable circular photogalvanic effect associated with left and right chiral spin helices in NiI2.
- **observation of interband collective excitations** — Experimental detection of interband collective modes in twisted bilayer graphene.
- **observation of isotropic and anisotropic g‑factor corrections matching theory** — The experiment found good agreement with theoretical predictions for both isotropic and anisotropic g‑factor contributions.
- **observation of ndr** — Negative differential resistance observed for the 400 nm and 500 nm quantum dots.
- **observation of orbital ferromagnetism in tblg** — Transport measurements reveal highly anisotropic orbital ferromagnetism below 30 mK.
- **observation of p‑wave magnetism in nii₂** — Experimental confirmation that NiI₂ hosts a p‑wave magnetic state with odd‑parity spin polarization.
- **observed cascade of phase transitions and dirac revivals** — Series of electronic transitions and Dirac band revivals in magic‑angle graphene (Zondiner et al., 2020).
- **observed correlated insulator behaviour at half‑filling** — Insulating state at half‑filling in magic‑angle graphene (Cao et al., 2018).
- **observed minimal moiré heterostrain (< ±0.3 %)** — Result reported from the aligned‑tip measurement.
- **observed slope ≈ 6** — Measured slope of bias versus photon energy around 6, larger than the expected value of 1.
- **observed unconventional superconductivity in magic‑angle graphene** — Superconducting state reported in magic‑angle graphene superlattices (Cao et al., 2018).
- **octupole‑driven magnetoresistance in antiferromagnetic tunnel junction** — Octupolar order yields a sizable magnetoresistance signal.
- **odd‑parity nature of spin polarization** — Robust odd‑parity of ⟨σz⟩ despite changes in periodicity and spin‑orbit coupling.
- **odd‑parity spin polarization detected by cpge** — CPGE measurements on a single spin‑helix domain reveal an out‑of‑plane spin component with odd parity.
- **odd‑parity spin polarization directly couples to ferroelectric polarization** — Evidence that spin polarization switches together with ferroelectric polarization.
- **odd‑parity spin polarization preserved with soc** — Odd‑parity character of spin polarization persists despite spin‑orbit coupling.
- **onset features are strongly smeared by temperature** — Model calculations show that increasing k_B T washes out the k‑resolved onset peaks.
- **optical phonon peak behavior with twist angle** — Optical phonon peak grows as 1/q₀² as the twist angle decreases.
- **optimal h‑bn thickness (8–9 l)** — Chosen barrier thickness to maximize optical and electronic signals while keeping the resonant peak detectable.
- **optimization criteria provide excellent guidelines for stm performance improvement** — General conclusion that the derived criteria are effective.
- **optimized design yields –198.6 db at 80 hz (≈6 orders of magnitude improvement)** — Quantitative result showing the magnitude reduction after optimization.
- **origin of magic angles** — Theoretical identification of the geometric origin of magic angles in TBG
- **output power scaling with capacitance** — Finding that the ANC300 output power scales linearly with the measured capacitance.
- **overnight cleaning with 24 nn over 10 µm × 10 µm removes pc residues** — Successful removal of polymer residues after prolonged low‑force AFM scanning.
- **pairing magnitude varies by ~50% along the trajectory with twist angle** — Observation that Δ changes significantly as θ (or Kθ) is varied.
- **parallel xmcd predicted for ruo₂ and nif₂** — Theoretical work predicts a weak parallel XMCD in these candidate altermagnets, unrelated to the macroscopic staggered magnetization.
- **peak current decreases after second etch** — Peak currents are reduced and valley currents increase after the second chemical etching.
- **perpendicular xmcd is weak and not generally applicable for identifying altermagnetism** — Perpendicular XMCD signals in MnTe are an order of magnitude smaller than parallel XMCD and vanish for certain crystal orientations.
- **phason damping by moiré disorder** — Low‑energy phason modes can be damped by disorder in the moiré superlattice.
- **phason epc scales with tunnelling** — The electron‑phonon coupling of the phason mode scales proportionally with the interlayer tunnelling amplitude.
- **phonon emission lines identified in second derivative** — Horizontal lines in the second‑derivative plot correspond to inelastic phonon emission.
- **phonon peaks at identical energy with asymmetric intensity** — Mirrored I‑V curves show phonon peak positions at the same energy while the intensities differ.
- **phonon wave vector equals distance between dirac points** — The phonon wave vector involved in inelastic tunneling is approximately the separation of the tip and sample Dirac points.
- **phonons of hbn barrier detected** — Momentum‑conserving tunnelling experiments have observed phonons of an hBN barrier between twisted layers.
- **phonon‑assisted tunneling** — Tunneling process assisted by emission or absorption of a phonon.
- **photocurrent amplitude decreases with temperature** — Photocurrent magnitude diminishes as temperature rises, indicating magnetic origin.
- **photocurrent jump between lcp and rcp appears below 60 k and vanishes at transition temperature** — Observation of a clear helicity‑dependent photocurrent jump in the multiferroic phase.
- **photocurrent jump between lcp and rcp below 60 k** — Observation of a clear photocurrent jump when switching light helicity below the multiferroic transition temperature.
- **photon count rate reduction per h‑bn layer** — Photon count rate decreases similarly with increasing h‑BN thickness.
- **photon emission strongest at θ = 0°** — Maximum photon‑emission observed for perfectly aligned graphene electrodes.
- **piezo rotator uhv advantage** — Piezo rotators avoid air leaks in UHV chambers, unlike mechanical rotators.
- **pl drop explained by exciton** — Interpretation that the photoluminescence drop at low twist angle is due to excitonic effects.
- **plaid‑like spin splitting in noncoplanar antiferromagnet** — Observes a distinctive plaid pattern of spin splitting.
- **plasmon breaks momentum mismatch** — Theoretical result that plasmon creation can bridge the parallel‑momentum mismatch and enable inelastic tunneling.
- **plasmon broadening at low q** — Broadening of the plasmon spectrum at small wave vectors caused by decay into particle‑hole pairs.
- **plasmon contribution to differential conductance** — Theoretical result that electron‑plasmon scattering adds distinct δ‑function features to dI/dV.
- **plasmon damping excludes twist‑angle regime** — When the tip Dirac point aligns with the γ point, small‑Q plasmons are damped, removing that twist‑angle regime from consideration.
- **plasmon dispersion around 40 mev** — Bright curve in the dissipative part of the screening potential tracing the plasmon dispersion near 40 meV.
- **plasmon dispersion traced by v*(θ)** — Bias dependence V*(θ) follows the plasmon dispersion relation.
- **plasmon energy (~40 mev) smaller than central‑band bandwidth (~50 mev)** — Result showing that the plasmon mode lies within the flat‑band manifold, allowing damping by particle‑hole pairs.
- **plasmon energy smaller than central‑band bandwidth** — Observation that the plasmon energy (~40 meV) is lower than the central‑band bandwidth (~50 meV).
- **plasmon frequency shows rapid increase then a flat plateau** — Predicted ω(q) behavior: steep rise at small q followed by a flat segment up to the Brillouin‑zone edge.
- **plasmon peaks unchanged away from γ point** — Numerical results show plasmon peaks are qualitatively unchanged despite tip screening.
- **plasmon peaks unchanged away from γ point** — Tip screening does not modify the plasmon peaks far from the Brillouin‑zone center.
- **plasmon pole at ~40 mev near mbz boundary** — Sharp peaks in the anti‑Hermitian part of the screening potential observed at ≈40 meV.
- **plasmon satellite intensity high at κ′, low near γ point** — Experimental result showing strong satellite peaks at κ′ and only weak kinks near the Γ point.
- **plasmon satellite peaks in di/dv** — Two satellite peaks at bias ≈ ±50 meV in the differential conductance, arising from inelastic tunneling involving plasmons.
- **plasmon satellites are much weaker than quasiparticle peaks at low bias** — Observation that plasmon‑induced features have orders‑of‑magnitude smaller amplitude than elastic quasiparticle peaks.
- **plasmon satellites observed at low bias** — Calculated appearance of dispersionless plasmon satellites at |eV| ≈ ħω_q.
- **plasmon wavelength shifts** — Observed shift of plasmon wavelength that depends on propagation direction.
- **plasmon‑assisted tunneling produces a kink in di/dv** — The onset of inelastic tunneling into remote bands appears as a weak kink in the differential conductance.
- **plasmon‑assisted tunneling reveals the flat part of the plasmon spectrum** — Theoretical result that tunneling into central MATBG bands maps the flat region of ω(q).
- **plasmon‑assisted tunnelling appears as dips in d²i/dv²** — Second‑derivative conductance maps show dips corresponding to plasmon‑assisted tunnelling.
- **positioners successfully tested down to 10 mk** — The positioners operate in dilution cryostats at temperatures as low as 10 mK with acceptable heat load.
- **possible dirac level splitting remains unjustified** — Proposed explanation lacks supporting evidence.
- **potential difference observed in efm images** — EFM images clearly show the electrostatic potential difference between a +5 V biased strip and unbiased strips.
- **potential for picosecond‑scale switching of helimagnetic order and low‑energy ferroelectric switching for spintronic devices** — Implication of the observed phenomena for energy‑efficient antiferromagnetic spintronic applications.
- **potential magnitude about twice theoretical prediction** — Measured potential amplitude is roughly double that expected from existing theory.
- **power dissipation calculation** — With C=150 nF, U=40 V, f=50 Hz the calculated power is ≈240 µW.
- **power drop 84 → 64 mw after 4.5 h** — Power decreased from 84 mW to 64 mW after 4.5 hours of work.
- **power stable 70–80 mw after periscope** — Power measured after the periscope remained between 70 mW and 80 mW.
- **pressure dependence of the magic twist angle** — Demonstrated that pressure reduces the required twist angle for flat bands.
- **pressure‑controlled anticrossing finding** — Evidence that increasing pressure drives an anticrossing between flat and remote bands.
- **pronounced conductance step at vb≈160 mv due to k‑point optical phonons (lo, to)** — A strong increase in G around 160 mV is attributed to intervalley longitudinal‑optical and transverse‑optical phonons.
- **proposal suitable for exploring a wide range of magnetic materials** — Theoretical result stating the model’s flexibility beyond quantum spin liquids.
- **pₓ and p_y pairings split traces due to c₃z symmetry breaking** — For pₓ and p_y pairings the C₃z symmetry breaking splits the momentum traces.
- **q factor ≈ 15000 after baking** — Measured Q factor of the quartz tuning fork after a 1 hour bake at 150 °C.
- **q factor ≈ 8000 after tip mounting** — Achieved Q factor of the quartz tuning fork after completing the tip‑mounting procedure.
- **q reaches ~8000 after steps** — After eight adjustment steps the Q factor of the QTF reaches approximately 8000.
- **qtf after vacuum break properties** — After breaking the vacuum the QTF resonance is ~32757 Hz, impedance ~91 kΩ and Q ≈ 12 000.
- **qtf in can properties** — Commercial QTF in its sealed can has a resonance of ~215 Hz, impedance ~17 kΩ and Q ≈ 55 000.
- **qtf with tip glued properties** — With a tip glued on one prong the resonance is ~32431 Hz, impedance ~114 kΩ and Q ≈ 10 000.
- **qtm can be used as a momentum-resolved probe of superconductivity** — Demonstrated capability of QTM.
- **qtm can identify whether pairing arises from flat f or dispersive c bands** — Capability to distinguish pairing origin.
- **qtm can measure momentum‑resolved plasmon dispersion** — Inelastic contributions to G(V,θ) encode the full ω(q) of moiré plasmons.
- **qtm current weighted by tunneling matrix element projecting onto sample top layer** — Finding about matrix element weighting onto top layer.
- **qtm detects rotational symmetry breaking via c3z‑related tunneling channels** — Observation that three symmetry‑related channels enable detection of C3z breaking.
- **qtm directly extracts pairing magnitude at each momentum** — QTM resolves quasiparticle peaks and determines Δ_Kθ, distinguishing symmetry.
- **qtm directly measures the superconducting spectral function along momentum trajectories** — Result of the theoretical framework showing momentum‑resolved access to the spectral function.
- **qtm established as a powerful tool for high‑resolution spectroscopic studies** — Conclusion that the Quantum Twisting Microscope enables measurements previously inaccessible.
- **qtm resolves specific tip rotation angles where a gap appears, distinguishing pairing channels** — Key advantage of QTM over STM demonstrated in the study.
- **qtm reveals nodal points in the superconducting order parameter** — Zero‑bias conductance peaks map the momentum locations of gap nodes.
- **qtm tip footprint** — Spatial extent of tip–sample contact estimated as ~50 × 200 nm.
- **quality factor calculation example** — Example quality factor Q ≈ 33 calculated from f0≈2.5 kHz and FWHM≈75 Hz.
- **quantization effect observed at 4.2 k** — I‑V measurement of a 450 nm dot at 4.2 K shows signatures of energy quantization.
- **quantum capacitance dominates low‑voltage range** — The model shows that the quantum capacitance term is the main contribution for small bias voltages.
- **quantum lifetime of carriers in double bilayer graphene** — Quantified carrier lifetime using quantum lifetime spectroscopy.
- **quantum twisting microscopy of phonons** — Direct imaging of phonon modes in TBG using the QTM
- **quasiparticle peaks suppressed near κ′ due to polarization toward bottom layer** — Observation that quasiparticle intensity drops near the κ′ point because electronic states are polarized toward the layer far from the tip.
- **rapid decay of |t|² for θ<0** — For negative twist angles |T|² decreases faster than the Bloch probability due to interference suppression.
- **rapid decay of φ₍q₎ with q** — Calculated electrostatic potential φ₍q₎ drops quickly as the wave vector increases.
- **rapid increase of di/dv near remote‑band edge** — Sharp rise of dI/dV at biases close to the edge of remote bands due to inelastic tunneling into flat bands.
- **ratio of intrasublattice and intersublattice tunneling parameters can be inferred from differential conductance** — Result showing that the tunneling parameter ratio is extractable when the tip Dirac point aligns with sample valleys.
- **record‑strength magneto‑electric coupling in nii2** — NiI2 exhibits a symmetry‑protected magneto‑electric coupling with record strength.
- **record‑strength magneto‑electric coupling in nii2** — Observation of exceptionally strong symmetry‑protected magneto‑electric coupling.
- **reduced di/dv along landau level gaps observed at b = 5 t** — Off‑defect conductance maps show clear suppression at the Landau‑level gaps of both top and bottom graphene layers.
- **reduction of tip‑sample distance response** — Observation that matching amplitude and phase of tip and sample reduces disturbance response.
- **relative intensities of electron and hole excitations encode bogoliubov coherence factors** — Intensity ratio provides direct access to the momentum‑dependent coherence factors.
- **reliable electrical control of spin chirality** — Demonstration that ferroelectric switching reliably controls spin chirality.
- **remote band features shift to higher bias (3.4°)** — Remote‑band signatures appear at higher bias in the 3.4° sample, often outside the measurement window.
- **remote bands resolved at charge neutrality** — High‑energy bands become clearly visible in d²I/dV² maps when μ_T = 0.
- **remote‑band quasiparticle peaks can be three orders stronger than kinks** — At higher bias, elastic quasiparticle peaks dominate over the weak inelastic kink by ~1000×.
- **resistive wires are not recommended** — A resistance of ~10 Ω can prevent the positioners from working due to the required high currents.
- **resistivity kinks (50‑100 k)** — Anomalies in graphene resistivity occurring between 50 K and 100 K.
- **resistivity of rotated graphite–graphene contacts** — Measured dependence of contact resistivity on rotation angle.
- **resolution limited by tip radius** — When the tip radius dominates (R ≫ d) the lateral resolution is set by R, not by the tunnelling distance.
- **resolution limited by tunnelling distance** — When the tunnelling distance dominates (d ≫ R) the lateral resolution is set by d and by the achievable tunnelling resistance.
- **resolution of the dual‑nature puzzle** — Demonstration that charge reshuffling underlies Dirac revivals.
- **resonance frequency increased** — Optimized design raised resonance from 7.683 kHz to 12.949 kHz; new design achieved 11.572 kHz.
- **resonance frequency shifts to ~32 khz** — Resonance frequency changes from 32768 Hz to around 32 kHz after the adjustment steps.
- **resonant peaks correlate with tmd exciton energies** — Observed dI/dV resonances appear at bias voltages matching exciton energies despite the TMD being outside the tunnelling path.
- **response decreases with frequency** — Tip‑sample distance response magnitude drops as excitation frequency moves away from resonance.
- **robust odd‑parity spin polarization in type‑ii multiferroic nii2** — Demonstration of a strong, non‑relativistic odd‑parity spin texture.
- **robust odd‑parity spin polarization observed in type‑ii multiferroic nii2** — Combined photocurrent and symmetry/DFT analysis confirm the presence of odd‑parity spin polarization.
- **room‑temperature magnetoresistance in all‑antiferromagnetic tunnel junction** — Demonstrates magnetoresistance persisting at room temperature in a fully antiferromagnetic stack.
- **rotating sample sweeps out‑of‑plane magnetic field** — Observation that rotating the sample changes the normal component of magnetic field.
- **rotator high‑field capability** — Attocube rotators have been used in magnetic fields exceeding 30 T.
- **rped combines magnetic information of xmcd with structural information of ped** — RPED simultaneously yields element‑specific magnetic and structural data.
- **sample outer‑cylinder bending resonance increased to 2.386 khz** — Result showing the sample bending frequency shifts upward in the new design.
- **satellites appear in the tunneling spectrum above elastic peaks due to plasmon emission** — Predicted signature of inelastic tunneling into Hubbard bands.
- **satellites in tunneling spectrum** — Additional peaks at biases above elastic tunneling due to plasmon emission.
- **screened interaction satisfies reciprocity condition** — Verification that W(k∥,z,z′,ω) = W(k∥,z′,z,ω) holds for the derived expression.
- **screening reduces pseudoelectric potential magnitude by ~2.2** — Self‑consistent Hartree calculations show that electronic screening lowers the amplitude of H0 by a factor of about 2.2.
- **sdh amplitude dependence** — Amplitude of Shubnikov‑de Haas oscillations grows with increasing magnetic field and decreasing temperature.
- **sdh oscillations appear versus effective magnetic field b cosθ** — Observation that plotting resistance vs B cosθ reproduces magnetic‑sweep SdH oscillations.
- **second coulomb diamond crosses at ~20 mev bias** — Finite‑bias crossing of the second CD.
- **second derivative of tunneling current probes dynamical spin structure factor** — The central result (Eq. 18) showing that d²I/dV² directly measures the dynamical spin structure factor with momentum and energy resolution.
- **selective molecule attraction to aa or ab regions** — DFT/TB calculations show the ESP tip of TBG preferentially adsorbs molecules on AA or AB domains.
- **self‑energy correction weak (≈1 mev shift)** — The self‑energy produces only a ~1 meV shift of remote‑band edges, negligible for the conclusions.
- **self‑energy encodes collective excitations** — The imaginary part of the off‑shell self‑energy contains information about the sample’s collective excitations.
- **self‑energy σ₁″ suppression near γ** — Self‑energy rapidly decreases near the γ point, indicating reduced electron‑plasmon coupling.
- **sensitivity at 300 k** — Measured sensitivity of 8.75 μV / pm at 300 K.
- **sensitivity at 4.2 k** — Measured sensitivity of 97 μV / pm at 4.2 K.
- **sensitivity increase factor** — Cooling from 300 K to 4.2 K increases the sensitivity by a factor of ≈11.
- **series resistance values (1–15 kω)** — Extracted RS values across devices.
- **sharp drop in elastic current past v**** — Observation of a sudden decrease in elastic tunneling current when bias exceeds V**.
- **sharp spectral feature of remote bands (fig. 2a)** — Sharp feature in the remote bands used to determine the momentum resolution.
- **sharpest tip from compressed tbg** — The tip with smallest radius is obtained when TBG is compressed, producing flat bands and localized states.
- **shear‑mediated stabilization of spin spiral order in nii₂** — Observation that shear stabilizes spin‑spiral order in NiI₂.
- **sheet carrier density 1.88×10¹¹ el/cm²** — Result obtained from SdH analysis.
- **shg finding** — Second‑harmonic generation exhibits a sharp jump at the helimagnetic transition temperature.
- **signal improvement factors** — Current signal improved by factor 2–5 (<200 Hz) and 1.5–2 (>200 Hz); z‑piezo signal improved >5 up to ~14.
- **signatures of chiral superconductivity in rhombohedral graphene** — Experimental observation reported by Han et al. indicating chiral superconductivity in rhombohedral graphene.
- **signatures of c₃z symmetry breaking appear in the tunneling spectrum of strained ma‑tbg** — Theoretical prediction that strain‑induced symmetry breaking modifies Dirac‑point tunneling features.
- **signatures of graphene‑hbn photo‑tunneling at 3° twist angle** — Experimental observation of photo‑tunneling features in a graphene‑hBN‑graphene stack twisted by 3°.
- **signatures of graphene‑hbn photo‑tunneling at 3° twist angle** — Observed in FTIR‑QTOM experiment as orbital‑resolved bands and FFT‑resolved photocurrent.
- **signatures of tunable superconductivity in heterostructures** — Observation of a sharp drop in resistivity and a plateau in the I‑V curve below 1 K, indicating tunable superconductivity.
- **simulated tunneling conductance matches symmetrized experimental data** — Agreement between numerical spectra and measured dI/dV after symmetrization.
- **sine-square twist-angle dependence for longitudinal phonons** — dI/dV contribution ∝ sin²θ for LA/LO modes.
- **single broad peak** — Broad spectral peak observed in linecut B when the chemical potential lies outside the flat bands.
- **single particle splitting less likely** — Conclusion that a single‑particle splitting does not explain the observed ΔE = ‑15 meV feature.
- **single‑layer van‑der‑waals multiferroic demonstrated** — Experimental proof of multiferroicity in a monolayer van‑der‑Waals crystal.
- **single‑particle tunneling in graphene‑insulator‑graphene junctions** — Measured tunnelling current consistent with single‑particle theory.
- **singular tunneling current at dirac point crossing** — Current I ∝ |φ−φ_n| diverges when the Dirac point crosses the probed band (elliptical case).
- **singularities in tunneling current map momentum‑resolved energy dispersion** — Demonstration that bias‑voltage singularities follow the twist‑angle dependence of the sample’s dispersion.
- **singularities occur at distinct bias voltages with different intensities** — Each of the three singularities appears at a different bias voltage and shows a unique amplitude.
- **site‑resolved magnetic information achievable via rped** — RPED can separate Mn‑A and Mn‑B contributions because their diffraction patterns differ.
- **snr improves with thicker hbn** — Observation that signal‑to‑noise ratio increases when hBN is thicker, while amplitude decreases.
- **snr improves with thicker hbn, amplitude decreases** — Experimental observation that increasing hBN thickness raises SNR while reducing signal amplitude.
- **soft modes are present in magic‑angle twisted bilayer graphene** — Result reported in the 2020 arXiv preprint.
- **spectral feature at ν = ‑0.7 (fig. 4a)** — Sharp spectral feature used to determine the energy resolution of the QTM measurements.
- **spectral weight concentrated near γ at charge neutrality** — At ν = 0 the dI/dV intensity is localized around the Γ point.
- **spectral weight oscillates with periodicity δν≈1** — Weight of heavy‑electron bands repeats each integer filling.
- **spectroscopy invariant across momentum in flat‑band region** — Spectral shape is essentially unchanged for different momenta along the flat bands.
- **spike in photocurrent spectra** — Sharp feature attributed to Fano resonance, largely bias‑independent.
- **spin chirality reverses with electrical switching of ferroelectric polarization** — Observation that reversing the poling electric field changes the sign of the pyroelectric current, indicating reversal of spin chirality.
- **spin helicity observed by x‑ray diffraction** — Nonresonant circularly polarized X‑ray diffraction reveals spin helicity.
- **spin lifetime > 1 min at 0.6 t** — Record electron spin lifetime exceeding one minute in GaAs at ~0.6 T.
- **spin polarization reverses with chirality** — Spin polarization changes sign when the spin chirality is switched.
- **spin splitting scales with effective mass and wavevector magnitude q, unaffected by kondo coupling** — Theoretical result showing that spin splitting grows with effective mass and Q while remaining insensitive to Kondo interactions.
- **spins point purely out‑of‑plane, confirming g‑wave antiferromagnetic order** — Polarized neutron data show no in‑plane component, indicating c‑axis alignment.
- **spin‑splitting without soc finding** — Spin splitting can emerge even in the absence of spin‑orbit coupling.
- **spiral spin structures and skyrmions observed in multiferroics** — The 2020 paper reports the coexistence of spiral spin structures and skyrmions in multiferroic crystals.
- **splitting only in superconducting state indicates nematicity** — Observation linking splitting to nematic pairing.
- **stabilization time ≈40 s after pulsed field** — Zero‑bias photocurrent stabilizes about 40 seconds after a 12 MV m⁻¹ pulsed electric field.
- **stable and homogeneous photon emission** — Emission intensity remains constant over time and is uniform across the device.
- **stacking‑angle‑tunable photoluminescence demonstrated** — Photoluminescence intensity varies with the stacking angle in twisted bilayer graphene.
- **step height in conductance proportional to epc strength** — The magnitude of each conductance step (or area under the d²I/dV² peak) directly reflects the electron‑phonon coupling of that phonon branch.
- **step in di/dv at the inelastic threshold** — Observed conductance step when eV_b equals ħω_{q0}, indicating phonon-assisted tunneling.
- **step size variation with temperature** — Measured step‑size ratio 41.5 : 2.6 : 1 at 300 K, 77 K and 4.2 K, larger than the calculated 5.5 : 1 ratio.
- **steps in coulomb‑diamond edges reflecting landau level gaps** — At 5 T magnetic field the diamond edges display discrete steps corresponding to Landau level gaps.
- **stiff‑and‑light design improves performance** — Making the STM as stiff and light as possible pushes resonance frequencies higher and improves noise performance.
- **stm achieved atomic resolution on si(111)-(7×7) in 1983** — First imaging of the Si(111)-(7×7) surface with STM demonstrated atomic resolution.
- **stm image relatively insensitive to atoms beyond the first layer for au(110)** — Calculated images show little change when deeper atomic layers are altered.
- **strain less than 0.1 %** — Upper bound on strain of the graphene layers inferred to be < 0.1 %.
- **strong electron‑phonon coupling in magic‑angle tbg** — Experimental evidence of sizable electron‑phonon interaction in TBG
- **strong inter-valley electron-phonon coupling in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene** — Measured large inter-valley electron-phonon coupling.
- **strongest features arise from tip fermi circle touching flat band** — The most prominent peaks in the d²I/dV² map are produced by the tip’s Fermi edge intersecting the flat band of TBG.
- **strongly coupled superconductivity in magic‑angle twisted trilayer graphene** — Observation of robust superconductivity in twisted trilayer graphene.
- **strongly enhanced tunneling current** — Observation that nesting of Fermi circles leads to a large increase in tunneling current.
- **structure in di/dvb at v*_b and v**_b** — The differential conductance exhibits distinct features at the two characteristic voltages.
- **subband‑resolved momentum‑conserved resonant tunneling** — Demonstrated tunneling that conserves in‑plane momentum between monolayer and trilayer graphene.
- **substantial decay of moiré potential** — Observation that the moiré potential amplitude decays by ~60 % when the height increases from 0.8 nm to 1.5 nm.
- **sub‑10 nm horizontal resolution** — Achieved lateral resolution better than 10 nm in cryogenic AFM images.
- **successful vis‑thz co‑alignment to csnom tip** — Result that the VIS and THz beams are correctly overlapped and focused on the tip after following the procedure.
- **sum rule holds well under weak strain** — Numerical results show that the sum of squared tunneling matrix elements is insensitive to ε=0.1% strain.
- **superconducting wires reduce heat transfer without increasing resistance** — Using superconducting wiring is an effective way to lower the heat load on the cryostat.
- **superconductivity in rhombohedral trilayer graphene** — Superconducting phase in rhombohedral stacked trilayer graphene.
- **superfluid stiffness of magic‑angle twisted bilayer graphene** — Phase stiffness quantified for magic‑angle bilayer graphene.
- **superfluid stiffness of twisted trilayer graphene superconductors** — Measurement of phase stiffness in twisted trilayer graphene.
- **suppressed electron‑plasmon coupling near γ** — Imaginary part of self‑energy drops near Γ, indicating reduced electron‑plasmon coupling.
- **suppression of di/dv for |vb| < vth** — Observed strong reduction of tunnelling conductance at low bias, attributed to large contact resistance.
- **switching energy up to five orders lower than batio3** — Estimated switching energy of NiI2 is dramatically lower than that of conventional BaTiO₃.
- **s‑wave pairing yields coincident traces in qtm** — For s‑wave pairing the three momentum traces coincide.
- **technique extends to monolayer limit** — The tunneling‑spectroscopy approach works for single‑layer samples and provides momentum and energy resolution.
- **temperature reduces contrast of d²i/dv² vs v** — Finite temperature lowers the amplitude of the d²I/dV² feature without smearing its shape.
- **terahertz circular dichroism in commensurate twisted bilayer graphene** — Prediction of THz circular dichroism arising from the moiré superlattice symmetry.
- **terahertz evidence of electromagnon excitations in nii₂** — Detection of electromagnon modes in NiI₂ using terahertz spectroscopy.
- **theory captures experiment** — Theoretical calculations reproduce the main features observed in the measurements.
- **theory‑experiment agreement with no free parameters** — Calculated trajectories match the data without adjustable parameters.
- **thermal limit of frequency noise** — Calculated limit of frequency noise based on the ratio of thermal energy to stored mechanical energy.
- **thermal phonon absorption may dominate at low angles** — At 4 K, absorption of thermally excited phonons could explain the large conductive background at small twist angles.
- **tilt‑induced localization observed** — Localization and delocalization of electrons in the second Landau level under tilt.
- **tip becomes dirty after measurement** — Observation that the scanning tip is contaminated after a measurement run.
- **tip curvature can be tuned from ~30 nm to >500 nm** — Relationship between etching current and resulting tip radius demonstrated in Ni etching experiments.
- **tip renormalizes relative permittivity** — Tip screening changes εr → εr + πα_G/2.
- **tip screening does not qualitatively change tunneling spectrum** — Conclusion that dynamical screening from the tip does not lead to qualitative changes in the tunneling spectrum of MLG–TBG junctions.
- **tip screening more pronounced at small q** — Difference between screened and unscreened spectra is larger at small wavevectors.
- **tip screening more pronounced at small q** — The effect of tip screening is strongest at low wave‑vector values.
- **tip screening reduces plasmon energy** — Presence of the MLG tip lowers the plasmon energy and weakens the interaction mediated by plasmons in the sample.
- **to mode dominates epc** — Calculated EPC shows the transverse‑optical mode contributes most strongly.
- **to phonon has larger bare epc than lo near the k point** — Bare electron‑phonon coupling of the TO mode exceeds that of the LO mode close to the K point.
- **top and bottom gates enable quantum‑dot spectroscopy of local electrostatic potentials** — Result of the quantum‑dot‑assisted spectroscopy experiment.
- **total self‑consistent potential for v = 0** — Calculated potential for v = 0 reproduces the experimental C6 symmetry but with half the magnitude.
- **traces moved to the center plane (2)** — Observation that routing traces to the central layer improves layout.
- **transfer function magnitude improvement** — Optimized design shows six orders of magnitude higher transfer function at low frequencies compared with original.
- **transfer functions can be rewritten in terms of resonance frequencies** — Analytical insight that simplifies the design optimisation.
- **transition from abrikosov to josephson vortices** — Observed transition from slow Abrikosov to fast Josephson vortices.
- **transition near 59.5 k** — Clear change in zero‑bias photocurrent indicating the multiferroic transition.
- **transitions from superconducting to mott insulator and metallic phases** — Experimental observation of phase transitions in the TLG/hBN superlattice.
- **transport between twisted graphene layers** — Theoretical prediction of angle‑dependent interlayer conductance.
- **transverse phonons dominate inelastic tunneling at small twist angles** — At small twist angles, transverse phonons contribute more strongly to the inelastic tunneling current.
- **transverse phonons dominate interlayer coupling** — Theoretical result that transverse phonon modes give the largest contribution to the interlayer electron‑phonon coupling.
- **triangulation method can locate superconducting nodes in the brillouin zone** — Method provides node positions without prior gap knowledge.
- **triangulation method locates p* and gap** — The triangulation method can determine the momentum point p* and the superconducting gap |Δ(p*)| = |eV|.
- **triangulation method remains valid for superconductors with nodes away from ξk=0** — General applicability.
- **tunable correlated states and spin‑polarized phases observed** — Observation that correlated and spin‑polarized phases can be tuned in twisted bilayer‑bilayer graphene.
- **tuning of valley and chiral quantum state** — Demonstrated electrical control of valley‑polarised Dirac states.
- **tuning superconductivity in twisted bilayer graphene** — Gate‑controlled tuning of superconducting phase in twisted bilayer graphene.
- **tunneling conductance derived from spectral function** — The tunneling conductance is obtained from the spectral function via an approximate formula.
- **tunneling current proportional to surface ldos at tip position** — Core theoretical result linking measured current to the local density of states.
- **tunneling into a dispersive band displays higher sensitivity to the momentum dependence of the plasmon dispersion** — Observation that dispersive‑band tunneling is more responsive to plasmon momentum dispersion.
- **tunneling matrix elements become strain‑sensitive near dirac points** — When the tip momentum approaches the Dirac points, weak strain strongly modulates the tunneling amplitude.
- **tunneling‑current singularity induced by dirac points** — Singular contribution to the tunneling current that appears when the tip band possesses a Dirac point at p = 0.
- **twistable electronics with dynamically rotatable heterostructures** — Demonstrated functional devices whose performance changes with real‑time rotation.
- **twist‑controlled resonant tunnelling** — Demonstration that the twist angle tunes resonant tunnelling amplitude.
- **twist‑controlled resonant tunnelling demonstrated** — Resonant tunnelling currents are modulated by the twist angle in graphene/hBN/graphene stacks.
- **two flat bands with 35 mev gap at 1.1°** — At the magic angle the spectrum shows two extremely flat bands separated by ~35 meV, gapless near Γ.
- **two‑fold anisotropy of bc₂** — Large two‑fold angular dependence of the upper critical field in SrₓBi₂Se₃.
- **typical stiffness range** — Typical stiffness values are 0.05 N/µm for scanners and ≈1 N/µm for z‑positioners.
- **unconventional superconductivity in magic‑angle graphene superlattices** — Observation of superconductivity in magic‑angle graphene superlattices.
- **undamped plasmons persist near the mbz boundary despite metallic gating** — Result reported in the calculations.
- **van hove singularities at ±174 mev** — Remote bands exhibit van Hove peaks at +174 meV and –171 meV.
- **van hove singularity near 3 ev** — Experimental identification of a Van Hove singularity around the M point at ~3 eV.
- **vanishing cpge along ferroelectric polarization direction** — Photocurrent shows no discernible CPGE when the light polarization is aligned with the ferroelectric axis.
- **vanishing gap identifies a nodal point** — Observation of nodal point via gap closure.
- **vertex correction accounts for orange vs. blue curve differences** — Interlayer Coulomb vertex correction explains the discrepancy between calculations that include or omit it.
- **visualization of flat electronic band** — Real‑space imaging of the flat band near the magic angle
- **w0/w1 ratio = 0.6** — Best‑fit ratio of interlayer tunnelling parameters that matches the experimental Ipeak.
- **weak tip‑sample coulomb effect on plasmons** — When the tip Fermi energy is sufficiently close to the charge‑neutrality point, the long‑range Coulomb interaction between tip and sample has only a weak influence on the sample’s plasmons.
- **weak twist-angle dependence of intralayer coupling** — Hintra contribution shows little dependence on θ and is similar for longitudinal and transverse phonons.
- **widely tunable bandgap observed in bilayer graphene** — Direct observation that bilayer graphene exhibits a bandgap that can be tuned over a wide range.
- **za coupling ≈25 mev at 6°** — ZA mode coupling strength around 25 meV at twist angle 6°.
- **za energy decreases with smaller twist angle** — ZA phonon energy decreases as the twist angle becomes smaller.
- **za mode is gapped at small twist angles** — At small twist angles the out‑of‑plane acoustic ZA mode acquires a gap, cutting off its contribution to electron‑phonon coupling.
- **za mode stronger than zo mode away from charge neutrality** — The ZA mode becomes stronger than the ZO mode when the system is away from charge neutrality.
- **za mode weaker than zo mode at charge neutrality** — The lower‑frequency ZA phonon mode is weaker than the ZO mode when the system is charge neutral.
- **za–ta crossing near 6°** — ZA and TA modes cross near twist angle 6°.
- **zero mcd in mno** — RPED calculation for MnO yields vanishing magnetic circular dichroism, as expected for a conventional antiferromagnet.
- **zero-bias resonances enable geometric triangulation of nodal momenta** — Method to locate nodes.
- **zero‑bias conductance peak occurs when tip fermi surface crosses a nodal point** — Experimental confirmation of the geometric nodal‑point detection scheme.
- **zero‑bias photocurrent reflects ferroelectric polarization** — The intensity and sign of the zero‑bias photocurrent directly track the net ferroelectric polarization.
- **δe = -15 mev feature** — Spectral feature observed at –15 meV relative to the Fermi energy when the chemical potential is inside the flat bands.
- **δe feature not caused by strain‑induced splitting** — Analysis suggests the observed ΔE splitting cannot be explained by simple strain effects.
- **λ estimate for optical modes** — Estimated λ for optical (TO/LO) modes from measurements.
- **λ estimate for phason mode** — Estimated λ for the phason mode based on measured EPC at 6°.
- **μ_bg = – μ_tbg** — Charge neutrality leads to opposite chemical potentials in bottom and top graphene layers, matching experiment with V_B=0.
- **χ = 0 due to mirror symmetry at θ_c = 0°** — At zero twist angle the AA‑aligned layers possess mirror symmetry, forcing the parameter χ to vanish.
- **≈15 mev excitation observed across regions and samples** — A robust excitation around 15 meV appears universally in MATBG devices, independent of heterostrain.
- **≈2 å vertical resolution** — Achieved vertical resolution of about 2 Å.

### material
- **10 nm n‑type doped inas cap layer** — Top cap layer of the RITD structure.
- **10 nm undoped inas spacer layer** — Spacer layer separating the buffer from the double‑barrier region.
- **12‑fischer male a** — Fischer male connector type A used in Project 1.
- **12‑fischer male b** — Fischer male connector type B used in Project 2.
- **12‑fischer male c** — Fischer male connector type C used in Project 2.
- **1t‑tase₂ monolayer** — Monolayer form of 1T‑TaSe₂ used in STM experiments.
- **1t‑tas₂ few‑layer** — Correlated charge‑density‑wave material with Mott insulating behavior.
- **1 mω metal film resistor** — Feedback resistor in the transimpedance amplifier.
- **20 % hcl solution** — Hydrochloric acid solution employed for both deoxidation and electrochemical etching.
- **2d semiconductors** — Layered semiconductors (e.g., MoS₂) explored for plasmonic electrical detection.
- **2d tellurium** — Two‑dimensional form of tellurium.
- **2 pf capacitor** — Feedback capacitor in the transimpedance amplifier.
- **3/8" female npt connector** — Connector type on the compressor for water inlet and outlet.
- **3p+n+e 6h male connector** — Male connector complying with IEC60309, rated 32 A.
- **3 mil diameter pt90/ir10 wire** — A wire composed of 90 % platinum and 10 % iridium used as the tip material.
- **aba‑trilayer graphene** — Three‑layer graphene with Bernal stacking.
- **acetone** — Acetone solvent used in tip cleaning.
- **afm stainless steel housing** — Stainless‑steel outer shell of the AFM head.
- **agm battery** — Bateria d’absorbed glass mat (AGM) sense manteniment, una variant de bateria disponible per al sistema.
- **al108-a objective lens** — Objective lens with 10 mm diameter and 8 mm focal length.
- **al1210-a objective lens** — Objective lens with 12.5 mm diameter and 10 mm focal length.
- **algaas** — Aluminum‑gallium arsenide alloy forming barrier layers in the heterostructure.
- **algaas two‑dimensional electron gas** — AlGaAs‑based 2DEG forming the conductive channel of the Hall‑bar sample.
- **algaas/gaas heterostructure** — Aluminum‑gallium arsenide / gallium arsenide heterostructure used as a semiconductor platform.
- **aligned graphene/hbn moiré interface** — The atomically aligned graphene on hexagonal‑boron‑nitride interface that forms the moiré superlattice in the QTM tip.
- **all‑antiferromagnetic tunnel junction** — A tunnel junction composed entirely of antiferromagnetic layers.
- **alsb** — Aluminum antimonide material used in the quantum dot heterostructure.
- **alsb layer (6 nm)** — Aluminum antimonide barrier layer in the double‑barrier region.
- **alumina plate** — Alumina plate used as part of the coarse z motor stack.
- **aluminium** — Target material that is sputtered onto the sample.
- **aluminum foil** — Household aluminum foil used to make simple cantilevers.
- **ammonium persulfite** — Oxidizing agent (3 g per 100 mL water) used in the wet‑transfer bath.
- **anhydrous chloroform** — Solvent used to dissolve polymer during transfer.
- **antiferromagnet** — Magnetic material with antiparallel spin ordering.
- **antiferromagnetic tunnel junction** — A tunnel junction where the barrier or electrodes are antiferromagnetic.
- **argon (ar)** — Inert gas used in processing steps.
- **argon gas** — Process gas used during sputtering.
- **aromatic hydrocarbons** — Molecular analogues of graphene that exhibit visible‑range plasmon‑like resonances.
- **attocube coarse‑motion piezo** — Piezoelectric element used in the tip assembly.
- **au(110) surface** — Gold (110) crystallographic surface used as a test case for STM theory.
- **au(110) surface** — Gold (110) surface studied with STM.
- **az125nxt** — Negative photoresist used for MLA trench.
- **barium gallium selenide (baga4se7)** — A barium‑based crystal suitable for mid‑IR generation.
- **beryllium copper** — Beryllium copper alloy (UNS C17300) used for bodies & contact of the connector.
- **beryllium copper** — Metal alloy used in the positioner body.
- **bilayer graphene** — Two stacked graphene layers; when twisted they form twisted bilayer graphene.
- **bilayer graphene** — Two stacked graphene layers forming the twisted bilayer graphene heterostructure.
- **bilayer graphene** — Two‑layer graphene system examined for its electronic properties.
- **binder** — Material used for the 14‑pin Binder connectors.

- **bi₂sr₂cacu₂o₈₊ₓ (bi2212)** — A high‑Tc cuprate superconductor mentioned in the supplemental references.
- **black phosphorus** — Layered semiconductor investigated for plasmons and interface polaritons.
- **blue tape** — Blue adhesive tape used for exfoliation.
- **bm24-10dp/2-0.35v(51)** — Component used on PCB 1 and PCB 3 for soldered legs.
- **bm24-10dp/2-0.35v53 connector** — Connector used on the pico_end PCB.
- **boron nitride (h‑bn)** — Hexagonal insulating crystal used as a spacer.
- **brass** — Alloy of copper and zinc used in the Swagelok adapters.
- **bridging graphite flake** — Graphite piece providing electrical contact to the cantilever line.
- **bscco** — A highly air‑sensitive high‑Tc superconductor mentioned as requiring low‑temperature exfoliation.
- **bulk material** — Three‑dimensional crystalline material.
- **cantilever** — AFM cantilever that carries the QTM tip.
- **carbon nanotube transistor** — A transistor made from an individual carbon nanotube.
- **carbon nanotubes** — One‑dimensional carbon structures.
- **carbon‑based materials** — Materials whose electronic structure derives from Dirac points, e.g., graphene.
- **carbon‑doped hexagonal boron nitride (c‑doped hbn)** — hBN material with carbon impurities providing low‑energy defect states.
- **ceramic bearings** — Integrated ceramic bearings that optimise load and runout.
- **chromium (cr)** — Metal used for the first deposited layer via electron‑beam evaporation.
- **chromium (cr)** — Chromium metal layer used in alternative evaporation.
- **chromium etchant (cr etchant)** — Chemical solution employed to dissolve chromium during the wet‑transfer bath.

- **coaxial shield** — Shield surrounding sensor coaxial cables, often made of stainless steel in cryogenic environments.

- **collector lens** — Short focal length lens to capture light from the LED, following Köhler illumination principle.
- **collinear antiferromagnet** — An antiferromagnetic crystal where magnetic moments are aligned antiparallel along a single direction.

- **copper** — Conductive material of the twisted‑pair wires.
- **copper** — Metal material comprising part of the coaxial wires.
- **copper (cu)** — Metal layer present in the graphene stack.

- **fischer female d** — Fischer female connector D used in Project 3.
- **fischer female e** — Fischer female connector E used in Project 3.
- **fischer female f** — Fischer female connector F used in Project 3.
- **flux** — Chemical agent in solder paste that cleans metal surfaces.
- **foam plate** — Soft foam pad used as a shock‑absorbing layer when handling positioners.
- **freestanding complex oxide membrane** — Thin oxide membrane that can be twisted to form oxide heterostructures.
- **ga ion beam** — Gallium ion source used for focused ion beam operations.
- **gaas** — Gallium arsenide semiconductor used for quantum dots and heterostructure devices.

- **gaas/algaas heterostructure** — Material system forming the two‑dimensional electron gas.

- **gasb** — Gallium antimonide material used in the quantum dot heterostructure.
- **gasb layer (5 nm)** — Gallium antimonide quantum‑well layer between the AlSb barriers.
- **gold** — Gold metal.
- **gold** — Metal coating used on tips for tip‑enhanced Raman spectroscopy.
- **gold (au)** — Noble metal used as an example where the atom‑superposition method works particularly well.
- **gold (au) electrode** — Metal electrode that provides a high electronic density of states.
- **gold electrodes** — Metal contacts transferred onto NiI2 flakes along specific crystallographic directions.
- **gold film (100 nm)** — Conductive gold coating providing low resistance and mechanical flexibility.
- **gold foil** — Metal foil used for the first AFM cantilevers.
- **gold wire (1 mil)** — Thin gold wire used to electrically link the tip to the quartz tuning fork.
- **gold‑coated afm tip** — AFM tip coated with gold for tip‑enhanced Raman spectroscopy.
- **gold‑coated tip** — Gold‑coated probe held in the TUNA tip holder.
- **graphene** — Two‑dimensional carbon lattice used in moiré superlattices.
- **graphene** — Graphene monolayer.
- **graphene** — Monolayer carbon lattice used in many of the cited studies.
- **graphene bilayer** — Two graphene layers stacked without twist.
- **graphene double moiré system** — Graphene encapsulated by two independent moiré superlattices.
- **graphene electrode** — Monolayer graphene sheet that serves as an electrode for tunnelling into the defect quantum dot.
- **graphene moiré superlattices** — Moiré patterns formed in graphene‑based heterostructures, leading to miniband formation.
- **graphene monolayer** — Single layer of graphene used on the tip of the QTM.
- **graphene nanodisk** — Patterned graphene nanostructure that supports localized plasmons.

- **graphene superlattice** — Graphene with a periodic potential leading to flat and dispersive minibands.

- **graphite** — Graphite material.
- **graphite electrode** — Graphite electrode used as the counter electrode during Ni tip etching.
- **graphite flake** — Thin graphite layer (10–50 nm) transferred onto the Pt pyramid to form the functional tip.
- **graphite flake (20–40 nm thick)** — Conductive layer placed directly on the Pt pyramid.
- **graphite rod** — Serves as the counter‑electrode in the tip‑etching cell.
- **graphite top gate** — Conductive graphite layer acting as the top electrostatic gate in the QTM junction.
- **hcooh (formic acid)** — Polar gas molecule used for FIR lines around 432.6 µm.
